description: Parameters for the UBI viability calculator. Based on Solow-Zeira task-automation framework and arXiv 2505.18687v3 modelling.

viable.label: UBI is mathematically achievable
viable.description: At this productivity multiplier and government revenue share, AI-generated output is sufficient to fund UBI at 11% of GDP without new job creation.

borderline.label: Borderline — structurally fragile
borderline.description: UBI is arithmetically possible but depends on sustained AI productivity growth and political will to maintain revenue share. Any shortfall in either dimension breaks the model.

notViable.label: UBI is not achievable
notViable.description: The productivity gains or government revenue share are insufficient to fund UBI at 11% of GDP. Displaced workers cannot be compensated from AI-generated wealth at these parameters.

productivityMultiplier.label: AI productivity multiplier
productivityMultiplier.tooltip: How many times more productive is AI-assisted labour compared to pre-AI baseline? A 3x multiplier means the economy produces 3x as much output with the same or fewer workers.

governmentRevenueShare.label: Government revenue share of AI capital profits (%)
governmentRevenueShare.tooltip: What percentage of AI-generated capital profits does government capture via taxation? Current effective rate is approximately 15%. Raising to 33% halves the required productivity multiplier.

counterintuitiveInsight: Competitive AI markets — where many companies drive down prices — reduce monopoly rents, shrinking the tax base available to fund UBI. Paradoxically, a concentrated, monopolistic AI market generates larger rents that are easier for governments to tax. The system that displaces workers may need to stay monopolistic to afford paying them.
_note: Viability thresholds derived from arXiv 2505.18687v3 (Solow-Zeira framework). Exact multiplier thresholds are model-dependent and should be treated as indicative ranges, not precise values.
